As polynomials in $n$, the expressions
$k!\ssp a_k(n)$
have integer coefficients for all $k \geq 0$,
and their leading coefficient is $5^k$.
plain_text: As polynomials in $n$, the expressions $k! a_k(n)$ have integer coefficients for all $k 0$, and their leading coefficient is $5^k$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Arithmetic/asymptotic statement (k! a_k(n) integral, leading coefficient 5^k) has clear combinatorial evidence and consequences for asymptotics; significant within the paper's enumerative framework.
viability_rationale: Strong local evidence: verified for small k, asymptotic/constructive lower bound gives 5^k leading term, and for fixed k the problem reduces to counting disjoint local defects—amenable to inclusion–exclusion/cluster methods.
rationale: The authors state as a conjecture that k! a_k(n) have integer coefficients with leading coefficient 5^k, and provide heuristic evidence but no proof.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1324-1330: 'Conjecture (Leading coefficient): As polynomials in n, k! a_k(n) have integer coefficients for all k≥0, and their leading coefficient is 5^k.'

title: Nearly Gorenstein normal graded rings
body_tex:
\label{b(R)<0} If $\chr(k) = 0$,  
$b(R)< 0$ and if $R$ has an isolated singularity, 
then $R$ is of strongly F-regular type.  
If, moreover,  $R$ is $\Q$-Gorenstein (that is, $r\; cl(K_R) = 0$ in $\Cl(R)$ 
for some positive integer $r$), then $R$ is log-terminal.
plain_text: If $(k) = 0$, $b(R)< 0$ and if $R$ has an isolated singularity, then $R$ is of strongly F-regular type. If, moreover, $R$ is $$-Gorenstein (that is, $r\; cl(K_R) = 0$ in $(R)$ for some positive integer $r$), then $R$ is log-terminal.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Relates the new invariant b(R)<0 to strong F-regular type and log-terminality, a significant connection in singularity/MMP theory; proven in special cases, general resolution would be impactful.
viability_rationale: Deep link between characteristic-0 invariants and F-regular type; proved in dim 2 and special cases, but full higher-dimensional Q-Gorenstein/log-terminal claim is a hard singularity theory problem.
rationale: Authors pose Conjecture 2.3 asserting b(R)<0 implies strongly F-regular type (and log-terminal if Q-Gorenstein); they prove special cases (dim 2, F-pure) but not the general statement.
evidence_snippet: Lines 509–514: Conjecture: if char(k)=0, b(R)<0 and R has isolated singularity then R is of strongly F-regular type; if Q-Gorenstein then R is log-terminal. Lines 516–523 note special-case proofs.

title: On Pseudo-Effectivity and Volumes of Adjoint Classes in Kähler Families with Projective Central Fiber
body_tex: Let $f: X \to S$ be a family from a normal $\Q$-factorial K\"ahler variety $X$ onto a smooth, connected, and relatively compact curve $S$. Assume that $X_t$ have canonical singularities for all $t\in S$. Then $K_{X_0}$ is pseudo-effective if and only if $K_{X_t} $ are pseudo-effective for all $t \in S \setminus \{0\}$. (By Theorem \ref{Theorem Ou25}, this is equivalent to the statement that $X_0$ is uniruled if and only if $X_t$ are uniruled for all $t \in S \setminus \{0\}$.)
plain_text: Let $f: X S$ be a family from a normal $$-factorial K\"ahler variety $X$ onto a smooth, connected, and relatively compact curve $S$. Assume that $X_t$ have canonical singularities for all $t S$. Then $K_ X_0 $ is pseudo-effective if and only if $K_ X_t $ are pseudo-effective for all $t S \ 0\ $. (By Theorem , this is equivalent to the statement that $X_0$ is uniruled if and only if $X_t$ are uniruled for all $t S \ 0\ $.)
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Asserts deformation stability of pseudo-effectivity/uniruledness for Kähler families without projectivity on the central fiber; leverages transcendental MMP advances and has notable consequences in complex geometry.
viability_rationale: Authors prove the statement when central fiber is projective and in dimension three; removing the projectivity assumption in general relies on broader transcendental MMP progress, so full resolution seems unlikely soon.
rationale: The paper proves the statement under additional hypotheses (projective central fiber or threefolds) and then explicitly proposes the more general deformation-stability conjecture without those assumptions, which remains open.
evidence_snippet: Lines 201–203 state the theorem with X_0 projective. Lines 211–213: "It is reasonable to propose the more general: \begin{conjecture} Let f:X→S... Then K_{X_0} is pseudo-effective iff K_{X_t} are pseudo-effective for all t≠0.\end{conjecture}"

title: On Pseudo-Effectivity and Volumes of Adjoint Classes in Kähler Families with Projective Central Fiber
plain_text: Let $f:X S$ be a proper surjective morphism from a normal $Q$-factorial generalized K\"ahler pair $(X,B + )$ onto a smooth, connected, relatively compact curve $S$. Fix a point $0 S$ and assume that the support of the boundary divisor $B$ does not contain the fiber $X_0$. Assume that the restriction to the central fiber $(X_0,B_0 + _0)$ is a generalized klt pair, such that $K_ X_0 +B_0 + _0$ is nef and big. Then $K_ X + B + _X $ is nef and big over $U$ for some open neighborhood $U S$ of $0$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Deformation stability of nef-and-big adjoint classes from a central fiber; meaningful for Kähler families but largely a local extension and follows from the Transbpf conjecture.
viability_rationale: Conjecture reduces to Transcendental BPF; holds for threefolds and nd≤3. Progress on TransBPF would resolve it, so near-term solvability mirrors that parent conjecture.
rationale: The conjecture (deformation of nef-and-big adjoint classes) is proposed in full generality; the paper proves it under a projective-central-fiber hypothesis and in several special cases, but not in general.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: If K_{X_0}+B_0+β_0 is nef and big then K_X+B+β_X is nef and big over a neighborhood. Proposition proves this when the central fiber is projective; general case left as conjecture.

title: Hodge theory of secant varieties
plain_text: Let $X$ be a compact pure-dimensional complex algebraic variety. Then $L_*(X)= IT _1 _*(X)$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Relates Goresky–MacPherson L-class to the intersection Hirzebruch class at y=1: a structurally meaningful characteristic-class Hodge index statement with broad consequences for singular-variety topology and Hodge theory.
viability_rationale: Longstanding Cappell–Shaneson characteristic-class Hodge index conjecture; only special cases known and no clear route to a general proof, so general resolution within five years is unlikely.
rationale: The paper states this general Cappell–Shaneson characteristic-class Hodge index conjecture and only proves it for secant varieties; the full statement for all compact pure-dimensional varieties remains unresolved here.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture stated (L_*(X)=IT_1_*(X)). Paper notes they will prove the conjecture for secant varieties ("We will use the above remark to prove Conjecture ... for secant varieties").

title: Clonoids over vector spaces
body_tex:
\label{conjecture:main}
Let $\algA$ and $\algB$ be finite modules. Then, there are only finitely many clonoids from $\algA$ to $\algB$, if and only if $\algA$ and $\algB$ are of coprime order.
plain_text: Let $$ and $$ be finite modules. Then, there are only finitely many clonoids from $$ to $$, if and only if $$ and $$ are of coprime order.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A natural classification conjecture in clonoid/minion theory: finiteness characterized by coprime module orders. Significant within universal algebra and CSP-related applications but limited to that domain.
viability_rationale: Significant partial results (vector spaces, distributive modules) and the necessary direction known; concrete reductions suggest a plausible, though nontrivial, near-term resolution.
rationale: The authors pose the finiteness iff coprime-orders conjecture for all finite modules; they prove many cases (vector spaces, others) but do not establish the full iff statement for all finite modules.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture 1.1: finiteness of clonoids iff module orders are coprime. Paper confirms it for vector-space source modules and other classes, but not in full generality.

title: Clonoids over vector spaces
body_tex:
\label{conjecture:strong}
Let $\algA$ be a finite abelian $p$-group, and let $\algR$ be a ring (considered as regular $\algR$-module), such that $p$ is invertible in $\algR$. Then, there is a $k\in \N$, such that $\mathcal O_{\algA,\algR}$ is uniformly generated by $k$-ary $(\algA,\algR)$-minors.
plain_text: Let $$ be a finite abelian $p$-group, and let $$ be a ring (considered as regular $$-module), such that $p$ is invertible in $$. Then, there is a $k $, such that $ O_ , $ is uniformly generated by $k$-ary $(,)$-minors.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A technical uniform-generation statement for operations on finite abelian p-groups (p invertible in R). Implies the main conjecture; useful but specialist and method-driven.
viability_rationale: Authors reduced to explicit finite p-group cases; elementary abelian and cyclic groups handled. Remaining small p-group cases are concrete and technically focused, making near-term progress plausible.
rationale: The uniform generation-by-k-ary-minors conjecture for p-groups is presented in the conclusion and is not proved here; authors note partial results and pose specific open cases (e.g. Z_{p^2}×Z_p).
evidence_snippet: Conjecture (Section Conclusion): for finite abelian p-group A and ring R with p invertible, O_{A,R} uniformly generated by k-ary minors. Authors state this is conjectural and ask about Z_{p^2}×Z_p.

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Proposes existence of energy-supercritical Type II blow-ups with precise asymptotics in high dimensions; important for supercritical blow-up classification yet remains a specialist, technically demanding PDE question.

title: Stability of natural bundles on curves
body_tex:
\label{conj-main}
Let $f\colon X \to Y$ be a finite morphism between smooth irreducible projective curves, and let $V$ be a general vector bundle on $X$. Then $f_* V$ is stable if the genus of \(Y\) is at least \(2\) and semistable if the genus of \(Y\) is \(1\).
plain_text: Let $f X Y$ be a finite morphism between smooth irreducible projective curves, and let $V$ be a general vector bundle on $X$. Then $f_* V$ is stable if the genus of \(Y\) is at least \(2\) and semistable if the genus of \(Y\) is \(1\).
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Beauville's conjecture is a longstanding, structurally meaningful prediction about stability of pushforwards linking Hurwitz spaces and moduli of bundles on curves; resolving it would have clear geometric consequences in algebraic geometry.
viability_rationale: Proved for the general member of any Hurwitz component, but Beauville's original assertion for every cover remains open with no explicit finite reduction—moderately unlikely to be solved within five years.
rationale: Paper proves the statement for a general morphism in any Hurwitz component but explicitly says Beauville's original conjecture (for every cover) remains open.
evidence_snippet: Remark: Beauville's original conjecture predicting the stability of the general pushforward for every cover remains open. (lines 1040-1042)

title: Spectral gap for Pollicott-Ruelle resonances on random coverings of Anosov surfaces

title: Vigemers: on the number of $k$-mers sharing the same XOR-based minimizer
body_tex:
Let $g : \Sigma^m\to\Sigma^m$ be such that $g(a_1\cdots a_m) = g_1(a_1)\cdots g_m(a_m)$ where $g_i:\Sigma\to\Sigma$. Then, by defining the minimizer $\min_g(x)$ of a \kmer $x$ as its leftmost \mmer $w$ that minimizes $\texttt{rank}(g(w))$ (for the lexicographical order), we can compute
$\pi_k^g(w) = \left\lbrace x\in\Sigma^k : \min_g(x) = w\right\rbrace$
in $O(|\Sigma|\cdot km^2)$ time and $O(km)$ space by adapting the equations presented in this article.
plain_text: Let $g : ^m^m$ be such that $g(a_1 a_m) = g_1(a_1) g_m(a_m)$ where $g_i:$. Then, by defining the minimizer $_g(x)$ of a $x$ as its leftmost $w$ that minimizes $rank(g(w))$ (for the lexicographical order), we can compute $_k^g(w) = x^k : _g(x) = w$ in $O(|| km^2)$ time and $O(km)$ space by adapting the equations presented in this article.
label: real_open_conjecture

title: Phase transitions with bounded index: Parallels to De Giorgi's conjecture
body_tex:
\label{conj:indimpends}
    Let $n\geq 3$, and let $u:\R^n\to[-1,1]$ be a finite Morse index solution to the Allen--Cahn equation. Then, $u$ has finitely many ends, in the sense that there is some $R_0>0$ such that $\{u=0\}$ has finitely many connected components in $\R^n\setminus \overline{B_{R_0}}$.
plain_text: Let $n 3$, and let $u:^n$ be a finite Morse index solution to the Allen--Cahn equation. Then, $u$ has finitely many ends, in the sense that there is some $R_0>0$ such that $\ u=0\ $ has finitely many connected components in $^n B_ R_0 $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A deep structural conjecture linking finite Morse index to finiteness of ends for Allen–Cahn solutions; resolving it impacts PDEs, geometric analysis and ties to De Giorgi/minimal surface problems, with broad consequences.
viability_rationale: Partial progress: n=2 is settled; this paper proves bounded-energy-density cases (n=4 and conditionally 4≤n≤7). Main gap is removing the bounded energy-density hypothesis and classifying stable solutions; active but nontrivial.
rationale: The finite-index implies finite-ends conjecture is stated as long-standing and not proved here in full; only n=2 is known and higher-dimensional cases remain open or conditional.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: finite Morse index solutions u:\R^n\to[-1,1] have finitely many ends. Paper notes n=2 is a theorem; higher n remain open/conditional.

title: Phase transitions with bounded index: Parallels to De Giorgi's conjecture
body_tex:
\label{conj:dPKW}
    If $u$ [a solution to Allen--Cahn in $\R^3$] has finite Morse index and $\nabla u(x)\neq 0$ outside a bounded set, then, outside a large ball, each level set of $u$ must have a finite number of components, each of them asymptotic either to a plane or to a catenoid. After a rotation of the coordinate system, all these components are graphs of functions of the same two variables.
plain_text: If $u$ [a solution to Allen--Cahn in $^3$] has finite Morse index and $ u(x) 0$ outside a bounded set, then, outside a large ball, each level set of $u$ must have a finite number of components, each of them asymptotic either to a plane or to a catenoid. After a rotation of the coordinate system, all these components are graphs of functions of the same two variables.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Classifies finite‑index Allen–Cahn entire solutions in 3D by geometric ends (planes or catenoids). It's a central structural conjecture in PDE/geometric analysis with clear consequences for phase‑transition anatomy.

Let $(M, \omega, \Omega)$ be a compact Calabi--Yau $n$-fold, where $\omega$ is a K\"ahler form and $\Omega$ is a holomorphic volume form. There exists a Bridgeland stability condition $\sigma=(Z,\cP)$ on the derived Fukaya category $\DFuk(M)$, whose objects are Lagrangian branes $\bL$, such that:

\end{enumerate}

title: Marvelous slices of orthogonal matrices

viability_rationale: Authors produced detailed SO(5) decomposition; remaining obstacle is degree-non-generic singular permutation intersections. Problem is concrete finite-dimensional and likely resolvable with targeted geometric/computational work.

title: Monomial bialgebras

title: On the Graphical $r$-Stirling Numbers of the First Kind for Specific Graph Families
body_tex:
\label{conj:r-monotone}
For any graph $G$ and fixed $k$, the $r$-restricted graphical Stirling numbers satisfy
\[
\st{G}{k}_1 \ge \st{G}{k}_2 \ge \dots \ge \st{G}{k}_k,
\]
i.e., the sequence decreases strictly as the size of the restricted set increases.
plain_text: For any graph $G$ and fixed $k$, the $r$-restricted graphical Stirling numbers satisfy \[ G k _1 G k _2 G k _k, \] i.e., the sequence decreases strictly as the size of the restricted set increases.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A natural monotonicity statement on r-restricted Stirling numbers; useful and plausible but largely a local combinatorial inequality without wide structural ramifications.
viability_rationale: Monotonic decrease as restricted set grows is combinatorially natural; likely quickly resolvable (proof or counterexample) since basic combinatorial arguments should settle it.
rationale: The monotonicity in the restricted parameter r is stated as a conjecture in the 'Further Work' section and is not established in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1811–1816: '\begin{conjecture} For any graph $G$ and fixed $k$, the $r$-restricted graphical Stirling numbers satisfy ${G\brack k}_1 \ge {G\brack k}_2 \ge \dots \ge {G\brack k}_k.$ \end{conjecture}.'

title: Newelski's Conjecture for $o$-Minimal and $p$-Adic Groups

viability_rationale: Kneser–Tits is a classical, long-standing conjecture. Known in many special fields (real, p-adic, real/p-adically closed); no clear route to a full general resolution in the near term.
rationale: The Kneser–Tits statement is presented as the classical conjecture; the paper notes it holds for R, Q_p, real- and p-adically-closed fields but does not claim a general proof.
evidence_snippet: Lines 809–816: statement of Kneser–Tits Conjecture and notes it is true for k = R, Q_p, real closed and p-adically closed fields, but presented as the conjecture.

viability_rationale: Active research area with partial and conditional results; authors obtain weaker bounds. Plausible further progress, but full uniform quantitative bound across all semisimple groups is nontrivial.

title: Totally $Δ$-Modular Tree Decompositions of Graphic Matrices for Integer Programming

rationale: The classical totally Δ-modular polynomial-time solvability conjecture is cited; known for Δ=2 but Δ≥3 remains open per the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 215–221: conjecture stated; next sentence: Artmann et al. solved Δ=2, but Δ≥3 remains open.

title: Comparison theorems for the extreme eigenvalues of a random symmetric matrix
body_tex:
\label{conj:nn}
Fix a subspace dimension $d$ and distortion parameters $\alpha = \beta \in (0,1)$.
There exists a construction of a random sparse matrix $\mtx{\Phi} : \R^n \to \R^k$
with at most $\zeta$ nonzero entries per column and with embedding dimension
$k$ that satisfy
\[
\zeta \leq \mathrm{Const} \cdot \alpha^{-1} \log d
\quad\text{and}\quad
k \leq \mathrm{Const} \cdot \alpha^{-2} \log d.
\]
For any fixed subspace of dimension $d$, with probability at least $1 - d^{-1}$,
this random sparse matrix $\mtx{\Phi}$ serves as a subspace embedding
with distortion parameters $\alpha, \beta$.
The constants are universal.

There exists a construction of a random sparse matrix $ : ^n ^k$ with at most $$ nonzero entries per column and with embedding dimension $k$ that satisfy \[ Const ^ -1 d k Const ^ -2 d. \] For any fixed subspace of dimension $d$, with probability at least $1 - d^ -1 $, this random sparse matrix $$ serves as a subspace embedding with distortion parameters $, $. The constants are universal.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Nelson–Nguyen conjecture asks for optimal sparsity and dimension for oblivious subspace embeddings; resolving it would significantly advance dimension reduction and randomized linear algebra with broad practical consequences.
viability_rationale: Paper already proves the lower-distortion bound; only the upper distortion remains. Active field with concentrated technical gap makes a resolution plausible within ~5 years, but nontrivial work remains.
rationale: The paper explicitly states it only proves the lower distortion bound and does not address the upper bound, so the full Nelson--Nguyen conjecture remains unresolved.
evidence_snippet: “So far, Conj has resisted all efforts. The next theorem provides the first complete proof that a SparseStack matrix with the conjectured parameters satisfies the lower distortion bound. This result does not address the upper distortion bound.”

title: Joint distribution of leftmost digits in positional notation and Schanuels's conjecture
body_tex:
\label{conj2}
If $b_1,...,b_n$ are integers $\geq 3$
such that no pair of numbers in the set 
$\{\ln b_1,...,\ln b_n\}$ 
are rationaly dependent, then 
$\frac{1}{\ln b_1},...,\frac{1}{\ln b_n}$ 
are rationally independent.
plain_text: If $b_1,...,b_n$ are integers $ 3$ such that no pair of numbers in the set $\ b_1,..., b_n\ $ are rationaly dependent, then $1 b_1 ,...,1 b_n $ are rationally independent.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Rational independence of reciprocals of log bases is equivalent here to the prime-log algebraic independence (Conj1); it is a nontrivial arithmetic rigidity with significant consequences in the paper's context.
viability_rationale: Equivalent to algebraic independence of ln p (Conj1), implied by Schanuel. This is a deep open transcendence problem; resolution in five years is very unlikely.
rationale: Conjecture 2 is presented and shown equivalent to Conjecture 1 (which is open); the paper does not prove it, so the exact statement remains unresolved.
evidence_snippet: Lines 370–376: statement of conjecture 2; Lines 379–381: "Conjecture 1 ⇔ conjecture 2," with no proof of either provided.

title: Antimagic labelling of graphs with maximum degree $Δ(G) = n - 4$

title: Antimagic labelling of graphs with maximum degree $Δ(G) = n - 4$

title: On Simon's third gap conjecture for minimal surfaces in spheres
body_tex: Let $M$ be a closed surface minimally immersed in  $\mathbb{S}^N$ such that the image is not contained in any hyperplane of $\mathbb{R}^{N+1}$. If $K(s+1)\leq K\leq K(s)$ for an $s\in \mathbb{N}$, then either $K=K(s+1)$ or $K=K(s)$, and thus the immersion is one of Calabi's $2$-spheres with the dimension of the ambient space $N=2s+2$ or $N=2s$, respectively.
plain_text: Let $M$ be a closed surface minimally immersed in $S^N$ such that the image is not contained in any hyperplane of $R^ N+1 $. If $K(s+1) K K(s)$ for an $s N$, then either $K=K(s+1)$ or $K=K(s)$, and thus the immersion is one of Calabi's $2$-spheres with the dimension of the ambient space $N=2s+2$ or $N=2s$, respectively.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Simon’s intrinsic gap conjecture for Gaussian curvature of closed minimal surfaces is a central, longstanding rigidity problem in differential geometry with clear structural and classification consequences if resolved.
viability_rationale: Decades-old Simon conjecture largely open; only s=1,2 resolved. Recent partial progress on s=3 (this paper) helps, but broad higher-s cases remain widely open—unlikely full resolution within five years.

title: On Simon's third gap conjecture for minimal surfaces in spheres

title: On Sets of Monochromatic Objects in Bicolored Point Sets
body_tex:
Let $n$ blue points, no three on a line, and $n$ red points, disjoint from the blue points, be given.
If the line through any two blue points contains a red point, then all $2n$ points lie on a cubic curve.

interestingness_rationale: Structural algebraic-geometric claim tying an incidence rule to containment on a cubic; proven for n=5, the general statement would strongly constrain constructions and bridge combinatorial incidence and algebraic curves.
viability_rationale: Recent structural conjecture building on Green–Tao methods; only small-n cases (here n=5) and examples on cubics known. Nontrivial global algebraic/ combinatorial obstacles remain, so moderate low near-term solvability.

body_tex: Let $M$ be a closed Riemannian $n$-manifold with $\operatorname{Ric}_g \ge -1$, $\operatorname{diam}(M) \le D$, and $\operatorname{vol}(M) \ge v > 0$. Then the first positive eigenvalue $\lambda_{1,1}$ of the Hodge Laplacian acting on $1$-forms admits a uniform lower bound depending only on $n$, $D$, and $v$.
plain_text: Let $M$ be a closed Riemannian $n$-manifold with $Ric_g -1$, $diam(M) D$, and $vol(M) v > 0$. Then the first positive eigenvalue $_ 1,1 $ of the Hodge Laplacian acting on $1$-forms admits a uniform lower bound depending only on $n$, $D$, and $v$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A central spectral-geometry conjecture controlling the Hodge 1-form gap under Ricci lower bounds plus noncollapsing; proving it impacts eigenvalue estimates and analysis on limit spaces.
viability_rationale: Honda–Mondino conjecture is open in higher dimensions and technically challenging; known results only in low dimensions and this work avoids the full conjecture, so near-term resolution looks unlikely.
rationale: The Honda–Mondino conjecture is explicitly stated and the authors note they do not resolve it in full; they study a related, restricted setting instead.
evidence_snippet: Lines 96-100 and 105-107: 'They proposed the following conjecture. ... In the present paper, our focus is not on resolving this conjecture in full...'

title: Remarks on modules of finite projective dimension
body_tex: There exists an integer \( e \) such that if \( M^{\otimes e} \) is torsion-free over an integral domain, then \( M \) is free.
plain_text: There exists an integer \( e \) such that if \( M^ e \) is torsion-free over an integral domain, then \( M \) is free.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Vasconcelos' conjecture on a uniform tensor-power criterion forcing freeness is a classical, deep homological question about rigidity of modules over domains with broad impact in commutative algebra.
viability_rationale: Vasconcelos's tensor-power freeness conjecture is longstanding with only partial results; it remains a hard commutative-algebra problem and unlikely to be fully settled soon.
rationale: The Vasconcelos conjecture is stated explicitly; the paper establishes special cases but does not prove the general existence of such an exponent e.
evidence_snippet: Lines 315–317 state Vasconcelos' conjecture; subsequent propositions prove only special cases (e.g., low-dimension or extra hypotheses).

viability_rationale: Strong low-k evidence and a clear local-independence heuristic; not a grand open problem but extending to all k may require new uniform combinatorial/probabilistic control, plausible but nontrivial within 5 years.

title: On Pseudo-Effectivity and Volumes of Adjoint Classes in Kähler Families with Projective Central Fiber
body_tex:
Let $f:X\to S$ be a smooth family from a K\"ahler manifold $X$ onto a smooth, connected, relatively compact curve $S$. Assume that $(X,B+\bbeta)$ is a generalized klt pair such that $(X,B)$ is log smooth over $S$ and $\bbeta =\overline \beta $ where $\beta$ is nef over $S$. Then the volume function
\[t\longmapsto {\rm vol}(K_{X_t}+B_t+\bbeta _{X_t})\]
is constant over $S$.
plain_text: Let $f:X S$ be a smooth family from a K\"ahler manifold $X$ onto a smooth, connected, relatively compact curve $S$. Assume that $(X,B+)$ is a generalized klt pair such that $(X,B)$ is log smooth over $S$ and $ = $ where $$ is nef over $S$. Then the volume function \[t vol (K_ X_t +B_t+ _ X_t )\] is constant over $S$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Invariance of volumes in smooth Kähler families generalizes Siu-type invariance of plurigenera; significant for deformation theory though technically tied to MMP machinery.
viability_rationale: Volume invariance proven in treated cases (threefolds) using MMP; general Kähler-family statement in higher dimension still open and tied to deep MMP/analytic issues.
rationale: The paper proves local constancy / invariance of volumes for Kähler threefold families (and related special cases) but poses the general volume-constancy conjecture for arbitrary Kähler families as open.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: For a smooth Kähler family with log-smooth generalized klt pair, t ↦ vol(K_{X_t}+B_t+β_{X_t}) is constant. Paper proves the threefold (and other special) cases earlier.

title: Antimagic labelling of graphs with maximum degree $Δ(G) = n - 4$
body_tex:
\label{conjalon}
    Let $G = (V,E)$ be a connected graph, with a vertex $x$ such that $d(x) = \Delta(G) = n - k$ for some $k \geq 4$. Then, if $n \geq n_0(k)$ for some $n_0(k)$, $G$ is antimagic.
plain_text: Let $G = (V,E)$ be a connected graph, with a vertex $x$ such that $d(x) = (G) = n - k$ for some $k 4$. Then, if $n n_0(k)$ for some $n_0(k)$, $G$ is antimagic.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Alon‑style large‑degree conditional: antimagicness for graphs with a vertex of degree n−k when n≥n0(k). Meaningful for large‑Δ cases but narrower than the global antimagic conjecture.
viability_rationale: Partial results (Δ≥n−2, Δ=n−3; here Δ=n−4 with m≥7n) exist but progress has been slow. Extending to all fixed k≥4 is broad and technical—modest chance of full resolution within five years.
rationale: The Alon-style conjecture for Δ(G)=n−k with k≥4 is stated; the paper proves a related k=4 result only under an additional m≥7n hypothesis, not the exact n≥n0(k) statement.
evidence_snippet: “Let G ... d(x)=Δ(G)=n−k for some k≥4. Then, if n≥n_0(k) for some n_0(k), G is antimagic.” (conjecture stated; paper proves k=4 with extra m≥7n)

title: Acyclic sets and colorings in digraphs under restrictions on degrees and cycle lengths

\]

interestingness_rationale: A universal asymptotic lower bound for acyclic sets in oriented graphs; strengthens tournament bounds and links sparsity to acyclic structure, a central problem in oriented-graph extremal theory with notable structural consequences.
viability_rationale: Strengthens a known hard tournament question (factor-2 gain); paper only achieves random-case results. Long-standing, nonlocal gap; unlikely fully resolved within five years.
rationale: Paper states the Aharoni–Berger–Kfir conjecture as motivation and only proves it for random regular oriented graphs w.h.p.; it does not resolve the general statement nor gives a counterexample.

viability_rationale: Paper gives a concrete mechanism and related asymptotic tools (Gale duality, moments); for fixed k this seems reachable via extreme-value/rare-event analysis, but conditioning on a rare event introduces nontrivial technical obstacles.

title: Duflo-Serganova functors and Brundan-Goodwin's parabolic inductions
body_tex:
\label{conj:BGN}
Let $\mathfrak g=\mathfrak{gl}(n|n)$, let $\mathfrak b$ be a Borel subalgebra, and let $\alpha$ be a
$\mathfrak b$-simple odd root. Then for $\lambda\in\Lambda$ one has
\[
\operatorname{DS}_{e_\alpha}\bigl(M^{\mathfrak b}(\lambda)\bigr)\ \cong\
\begin{cases}
M^{\mathfrak b_{e_\alpha}}\bigl(\operatorname{pr}_\alpha(\lambda)\bigr)\ \oplus\
\Pi\,M^{\mathfrak b_{e_\alpha}}\bigl(\operatorname{pr}_\alpha(\lambda)\bigr),
& \text{if }(\lambda,\alpha)=0,\\[2mm]
0, & \text{if }(\lambda,\alpha)\neq 0.
\end{cases}
\]
Here $\operatorname{pr}_\alpha$ is the natural projection on weight lattices associated with $\alpha$, defined
in \S6.

title: A Geometric Approach to the Links-Quivers Correspondence I: Rational Tangles
body_tex:
\label{LQCC}
   Given a link $L$ with $|L|$ components, if we set 
   \[
   P(L)=\sum_{j\geq 0}\left(\prod_{i=1}^j(1-q^{2i})\right)^{|L|-2}P^{\bigwedge^j}_L(q,a)x^j,
   \]
   then there is a symmetric quiver $Q_L$ such that $P(L)$ and $P_{Q_L}(\overline{x})$ are equal after a change of variables.

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A structural bridge between colored HOMFLY-PT link invariants and symmetric quiver/DT generating functions; proving it broadly would deeply connect knot theory with enumerative/quiver geometry and impact multiple active research directions.
viability_rationale: Proven for rational tangles/links (SW21 and this work); significant local geometric progress exists, but the full Links–Quivers statement for arbitrary links is broad and lacks a clear global reduction—unlikely in five years.
rationale: The Links–Quivers Correspondence is stated as a general conjecture for all links; only special cases (rational links) have been proved (Stošić–Wedrich), so the full statement remains open.

The Links-Quivers Correspondence could be phrased as follows. ... In

title: Borel and shifted category O

\label{charexp} For $w\in W$, $i\in I$ and $a\in\mathbb{C}^*$, we have
$$
\chi(L(\bpsi_{w(\omega_i^\vee),a})) = \chi^{w(\omega_i^\vee)} \chi_{w(\omega_i^\vee)}.
$$
plain_text: For $w W$, $i I$ and $a^*$, we have $$ (L(_ w(_i^),a )) = ^ w(_i^) _ w(_i^) . $$
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A precise character formula linking Borel and shifted category O; central to QQ‑systems and representation theory of quantum affine Borel algebras, with clear structural consequences within the subfield.
viability_rationale: Special-case (simple reflections) is proved and the conjecture is reduced to equivalent statements, but extending to all Weyl elements remains a substantial technical hurdle; moderate near-term solvability.
rationale:
The paper states Conjecture 
\ref{charexp} and only verifies it for w = s_i; no general proof is given here.
evidence_snippet: Lines 2133–2136: conjecture statement. Lines 2140–2143: established for w = s_i, but not in general.

title: Burning rooted graph products
body_tex:
\label{conj: tooth greedy}
If $n \le m$, then
\[
b(C_{n,m}) = \min\left\{T_\gr^{(S)} : S = 1,2,\ldots,n\right\}.
\]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Conjectured optimality of a specific greedy algorithm for combs in the tooth-dominant regime is a useful algorithmic/local claim but is a narrow, technically focused statement.
viability_rationale: Specific optimality-of-greedy claim for comb graphs in a narrow regime; authors have strong structural analysis and examples. Likely resolvable (proof or counterexample) in next five years.
rationale: This paper explicitly poses optimality of the greedy algorithm for combs (n ≤ m) as a conjecture and does not prove it; it remains an open specific statement.
evidence_snippet: Lines 2481-2489: 'We conjecture that a greedy algorithm always yields an optimal solution: Conjecture: If n ≤ m, then b(C_{n,m}) = min{T_gr^{(S)}: S=1,...,n}.'

title: Burning rooted graph products
body_tex: If $T$ is a tree, then $b(T) \le \lceil \sqrt n \rceil$.
plain_text: If $T$ is a tree, then $b(T) n $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The tree-specialization suffices to imply BNC and focuses a hard global conjecture to a natural, structured class; meaningful to specialists but narrower than the full conjecture.
viability_rationale: Long-standing Burning Number Conjecture for trees remains open despite many resolved special classes (paths, caterpillars, spiders); current paper handles combs only and gives no clear general breakthrough, so low 5-year solvability.
rationale: The tree-version is posed in the paper as a sufficient (still-open) conjecture to imply BNC; only special classes of trees are resolved, not the general tree case.
evidence_snippet: As such, the BNC can reduce to showing b(T) ≤ ⌈√n⌉ for T any spanning tree. Hence, a sufficient conjecture to establish the BNC is: If T is a tree, then b(T) ≤ ⌈√n⌉.

title: Surface topology and incidence theorems over division rings
body_tex: Let $\mathcal T$ be a simple, $(n+1)$-degenerate tiling of a surface of positive genus, and let $\D$ be a division ring. Then the incidence theorem corresponding to $\mathcal T$ holds in $\DP^n$ if and only if $\D$ is commutative.
plain_text: Let $ T$ be a simple, $(n+1)$-degenerate tiling of a surface of positive genus, and let $$ be a division ring. Then the incidence theorem corresponding to $ T$ holds in $^n$ if and only if $$ is commutative.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Links surface topology of tilings to algebraic commutativity of division rings, giving a deep geometric–algebraic dichotomy; significant within incidence geometry and noncommutative projective theory.
viability_rationale: Authors give constructive counterexamples and an inductive 3-degenerate method, plus genus-dependent degeneracy bounds. Extension to all (n+1)-degenerate tilings seems plausible but nontrivial, so moderate near-term solvability likelihood.
rationale: The author explicitly states this as a conjecture (stronger version of a proved result) about degenerate tilings and division rings; it is not proved in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: Let 𝒯 be a simple, (n+1)-degenerate tiling of a surface of positive genus, and let 𝔻 be a division ring. Then the incidence theorem ... holds in ℙ𝔻^n iff 𝔻 is commutative.

title: Surface topology and incidence theorems over division rings
body_tex:
For any $g > 0$, there exists a constant $n_g$ such that, for any simple tiling $\mathcal T$ of a genus $g$ surface, the corresponding  incidence theorem over a division ring $\D$ is equivalent to commutativity of $\D$
in any dimension $n \geq n_g$. In particular, one has $n_1 = 3$, so that any theorem in dimension $n \geq 3$ corresponding to a tiling of the torus holds over a division ring $\D$ if and only if $\D$ is commutative.
plain_text: For any $g > 0$, there exists a constant $n_g$ such that, for any simple tiling $ T$ of a genus $g$ surface, the corresponding incidence theorem over a division ring $$ is equivalent to commutativity of $$ in any dimension $n n_g$. In particular, one has $n_1 = 3$, so that any theorem in dimension $n 3$ corresponding to a tiling of the torus holds over a division ring $$ if and only if $$ is commutative.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A uniform genus-version that would tie genus to minimal ambient dimension for commutativity equivalence; broader and more structural than single examples but still specialized to incidence/topology interaction.
viability_rationale: Paper provides partial results, reductions to k-degeneracy and explicit torus evidence (n1=3); plausible but needs nontrivial combinatorial/topological extensions for all genera, so medium near-term solvability.
rationale: Presented as a conjectural consequence (existence of n_g with n_1 = 3) of the previous conjecture; the paper does not prove this general statement.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: For any g>0, there exists a constant n_g such that, for any simple tiling 𝒯 of a genus g surface, the corresponding incidence theorem over a division ring 𝔻 is equivalent to commutativity of 𝔻 in any dimension n ≥ n_g. In particular, n_1 = 3.

viability_rationale: Two-dimensional dynamical system; authors reduced cases to concrete parameter regimes. Remaining stability amounts to eigenvalue/linearization checks—tractable with standard ODE/dynamical-system methods.

title: Improved, sublinear projective Schwartz-Zippel and (sub)quadratic dimension growth bounds in arbitrary codimension
body_tex:
\label{SLSZ}
        A projective variety of degree $d$ and pure dimension $k$ in $\PP_\QQ^n$ contains no more than $cd^\frac{n}{n+1}B^{k+1}$ points of height $\leq B$ for some constant $c$ depending only on $n$ and $k$.
plain_text: A projective variety of degree $d$ and pure dimension $k$ in $_^n$ contains no more than $cd^n n+1 B^ k+1 $ points of height $ B$ for some constant $c$ depending only on $n$ and $k$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A sublinear degree exponent in projective Schwartz–Zippel would be optimal (matching lower bounds) and significantly improve rational-point counting/dimension-growth results, making it a substantial advance in arithmetic geometry/counting.
viability_rationale: Significant partial progress: proved for unions of linear varieties, n=2 fully, n=3 up to log factors, and several low-dim cases. Full arbitrary n,k likely needs new ideas, so moderate near-term solvability.
rationale: The authors formulate the sublinear projective Schwartz–Zippel as a conjecture and prove various special cases and near-results, but do not establish the full conjecture here.
evidence_snippet: “Conjecture (Sublinear projective Schwartz-Zippel): A projective variety ... contains no more than c d^{n/(n+1)} B^{k+1} points ...” followed by proofs only in special cases (lines 448–452).

title: Variants of Merge-Width and Applications

interestingness_rationale: Equivalence of bounded merge-width and universal merge-width would unify variants and boost structural and algorithmic consequences for graph classes; known in key subclasses but nontrivial in full generality.
viability_rationale: Conjecture holds for bounded expansion, bounded twin-width, and is stable under FO transductions per the paper; promising partial progress but general case requires new ideas, so moderate near-term solvability chance.
rationale: The paper explicitly conjectures equivalence (bounded merge-width implies bounded universal merge-width) and only verifies it in special cases; the general statement is not proved here.
evidence_snippet: “We conjecture the following. Every class of bounded merge-width has bounded universal merge-width.” (lines 889–893), with only corroborating special-case results given afterwards.

title: A Perfectoid Duality Between M-Theory and F-Theory
plain_text: Let \(X\) be a perfectoid boundary carrier equipped with a condensed sheaf/module \(F\) of sources/operators and let \(X^ \) be its tilt, assume the holographic generating functional is defined as a derived-limit object on \(X\), equation Z_ X [J]:=\!(-W_ X [J]), W_ X [J] R\!_ (W_ [J_ ]+S_ ct [,J_ ]), equation as in Sec.~, and posit that there exists a functorial correspondence equation T\:(X,F,J) (X^ ,F^ ,J^ ) equation such that, for a suitable class of observables \(O\), equation O_ X \ \ O^ _ X^ equation with matching scaling exponents and monodromy/duality actions transported through \(T\). Eq. is the precise statement that \(p\)-adic holography should be an arithmetic avatar of tower-completed Archimedean holography, with tilting providing the canonical bridge at the level of boundary data. Why old methods were definitionally fragile\:classical AdS/CFT has no intrinsic operation relating Archimedean and non-Archimedean boundary geometries and therefore cannot state - as a background-level correspondence. A minimal checkable milestone is the two-point kinematic scaling\:for a scalar primary \(O_ \) one has in Archimedean CFT equation O_ (x)O_ (y)_ X |x-y|^ -2 , equation while \(p\)-adic AdS/CFT yields equation O_ (x)O_ (y)_ X^ |x-y|_ p ^ -2 , equation and the Program is to realize as the tilted avatar of tower-completed boundary data whose harmonic analysis is governed by \(X\) and \( X^ \) .

title: On moduli of Fano varieties: an introduction to K-stability and K-moduli

rationale: The survey presents "GIT = K stability for cubic hypersurfaces" as a conjecture. Known cases (cubic threefolds, fourfolds, surfaces) are proved, but the general statement remains open.

title: No-dimensional results of combinatorial convexity. Dimension strikes back
body_tex:
For any fixed integer $k\ge 2,$
\[
\operatorname{Tv}(X,k,n)  \to 0
\]
as $n \to \infty$ if and only if $X$ is of non-trivial type.
plain_text: For any fixed integer $k 2,$ \[ Tv(X,k,n) 0 \] as $n $ if and only if $X$ is of non-trivial type.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Proposes an iff linking no-dimensional Tverberg behavior to Banach-space non-trivial type, a clear conceptual bridge between combinatorial convexity and Banach geometry with substantial structural consequences in both areas.
viability_rationale: Substantial partial results: no-dimensional Tverberg holds in spaces of non-trivial type, and constructions indicate failure for trivial type. Remaining quantitative/converse gaps are nontrivial but plausibly resolvable in the near term.
rationale: The equivalence (Tv(X,k,n)→0 iff X has non-trivial type) is posed explicitly as a conjecture in the paper and not resolved here; prior partial results discussed but not full equivalence.
evidence_snippet: Lines 447-452: "For any fixed integer k≥2, Tv(X,k,n) → 0 as n → ∞ if and only if X is of non-trivial type."

title: The optimal hypercontractive constants for $\mathbb{Z}_3$ and biased Bernoulli random variables
body_tex: One may conjecture that, for  $n\ge 2$,  the equality $r_{p,q}(\mathbb{Z}_n) = \sqrt{(p-1)/(q-1)}$  holds if and only if $\mathbb{Z}_n$ does not admit  nontrivial $(p,q)$-critical extremizer.
plain_text: One may conjecture that, for $n 2$, the equality $r_ p,q (Z_n) = (p-1)/(q-1)$ holds if and only if $Z_n$ does not admit nontrivial $(p,q)$-critical extremizer.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Conceptual link between existence of nontrivial critical extremizers and sharpness of the elementary sqrt((p-1)/(q-1)) bound on Z_n; impacts understanding of when classical bounds are exact.
viability_rationale: Authors resolve Z3 and Z2 case is classical; methods appear adaptable. Significant structural complexity for general n remains, so a near-term resolution is plausible but not highly likely without new ideas.
rationale: The paper poses the equivalence for general Z_n as a conjecture; the authors resolve Z_3 but do not prove or disprove the stated if-and-only-if for all n.
evidence_snippet: One may conjecture that, for n >= 2, the equality r_{p,q}(Z_n) = sqrt((p-1)/(q-1)) holds if and only if Z_n does not admit nontrivial (p,q)-critical extremizer.

title: The optimal hypercontractive constants for $\mathbb{Z}_3$ and biased Bernoulli random variables

interestingness_rationale: Asserting that optimal hypercontractive constants depend only on the smallest atom mass would markedly simplify classification for biased Bernoulli measures; significant within hypercontractivity though confined to this setting.
viability_rationale: Numerical evidence and Wolff's theorem suggest two-point extremizers; plausible symmetrization/convexity routes exist, but a uniform proof for all (p,q) and finite measures remains nontrivial.
rationale: The authors formulate this equality conjecture for biased measures based on simulations; no proof or disproof is provided in the paper.

title: Serre depth and local cohomology
body_tex:
\label{ConjG}
		Let $(R,\m)$ be a Noetherian local ring or a standard graded $K$-algebra. Let $I\subset R$ be an ideal, which we assume is homogeneous if $R$ is a $K$-algebra. Then
		$$
		S_r\text{-}\!\depth(R/I^{k+1})\ =\ S_r\text{-}\!\depth(R/I^k),
		$$
		for all $r\ge1$ and all $k\gg0$.
plain_text: Let $(R,)$ be a Noetherian local ring or a standard graded $K$-algebra. Let $I R$ be an ideal, which we assume is homogeneous if $R$ is a $K$-algebra. Then $$ S_r-\!(R/I^ k+1 )\ =\ S_r-\!(R/I^k), $$ for all $r1$ and all $k0$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Natural broad generalization of Brodmann-type stability to Serre depths; proven in key cases (monomial, low dimension) but the full asymptotic behaviour for arbitrary ideals has clear structural consequences in commutative algebra.
viability_rationale: Significant partial results (monomial case, dim≤3, many structural reductions) and new methods (Presburger arithmetic) give a realistic pathway; full generality still nontrivial but plausibly reachable within 5 years.
rationale: The paper states Conjecture G for arbitrary Noetherian local or standard graded rings but only proves several special cases (monomial ideals, dim ≤3, etc.), leaving the general statement unresolved.
evidence_snippet: Lines 258-266: 'we expect: Conjecture ... S_r-depth(R/I^{k+1}) = S_r-depth(R/I^k) for all r≥1 and all k≫0.' Theorem F (lines 246-251) proves the monomial polynomial case.

title: The realization problem of essential surfaces in knot exteriors
body_tex:
\label{strong}
For any even integer $b \ge 6$, the triple $(0, b, 1)$ is not realizable.
plain_text: For any even integer $b 6$, the triple $(0, b, 1)$ is not realizable.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A strong form of the cabling/realization obstruction for planar essential surfaces; connects to the Cabling Conjecture and has substantial consequences in 3-manifold and knot theory, making it a central open problem.
viability_rationale: Stronger than the celebrated Cabling Conjecture; only small cases (b=1,2,4) are known. No concrete new route presented; long-standing Dehn surgery problem unlikely to be resolved within five years.
rationale: The Strong Cabling Conjecture is stated as an open conjecture in the introduction; the paper does not prove or refute it and instead proves other existence results.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture [Strong Cabling]: 'For any even integer b ≥ 6, the triple (0, b, 1) is not realizable.' (lines 197–201)

title: Pruning distance of upset-decomposable persistence modules
body_tex:
\label{conjecture}
Let~$M$ and~$N$ be pfd modules with~$r = \operatorname{supdim} M<\infty$.
Then
\[
d_P(M,N)\leq d_I(M,N)\leq 2r\,d_P(M,N).
\]
plain_text: Let~$M$ and~$N$ be pfd modules with~$r = supdim M<$. Then \[ d_P(M,N) d_I(M,N) 2r\,d_P(M,N). \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Asserts Lipschitz equivalence between pruning and interleaving distances—central to stability and metric comparisons in persistence theory, with clear structural and practical consequences if resolved.
viability_rationale: Active area with partial results (including this paper's sharp bound for upset-decomposable modules); full general Lipschitz equivalence to interleaving distance remains nontrivial but plausibly resolved in coming years.
rationale: The conjecture (d_P ≤ d_I ≤ 2r d_P for pfd modules) is stated and discussed as open; this paper proves related results for upset-decomposable modules but not the full general statement.
evidence_snippet: Lines 438–445 and 447–452: Conjecture states d_P(M,N) ≤ d_I(M,N) ≤ 2r d_P(M,N). Paper notes left inequality was partially verified elsewhere and extension remains open; authors prove results for upset-decomposable modules only.

title: Half-periodicity of Zamolodchikov periodic cluster algebras
body_tex:
Let $(\Gamma, \Delta)$ be an admissible Dynkin biagram with $r$ vertices.
    The number of $\Gamma$-mutations in one period is the total number of roots in the root systems associated to each $\Gamma$-component, $h_{\Gamma}\cdot r$.
    Similarly, the number of $\Delta$-mutations in one period is the total number of roots in the root systems associated to each $\Delta$-component, $h_{\Delta}\cdot r$.
plain_text: Let $(, )$ be an admissible Dynkin biagram with $r$ vertices. The number of $$-mutations in one period is the total number of roots in the root systems associated to each $$-component, $h_ r$. Similarly, the number of $$-mutations in one period is the total number of roots in the root systems associated to each $$-component, $h_ r$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Combinatorial conjecture linking colored mutation counts to root numbers/Coxeter data in Zamolodchikov-periodic cluster algebras; a neat structural pattern but narrowly scoped within cluster combinatorics.
viability_rationale: Substantial progress in the paper (half-periodicity proved and several Dynkin cases verified); the conjecture is combinatorial/finite-case in nature, so completion appears likely soon.
rationale: The conjecture is stated and is only verified in the paper under a certain global ordering/certain cases; the full general statement is not proved here.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture (1151–1156) asserts counts of Γ-/Δ-mutations equal h_Γ·r and h_Δ·r. Corollary (1158–1164) verifies it under a specific global ordering only.

title: Research Report: some constructions in Polyhedral Symplectic Topology suggested by AI
body_tex:
Let $\Gamma_{v,\delta} \subset (S^3,\xi_{\mathrm{std}})$ be the Legendrian link obtained by intersecting a locally smoothed valence--$4$ vertex with a sufficiently small sphere. Then the following hold:

\smallskip

\noindent
\textup{(i)} If the polyhedral Maslov index satisfies 
\[
\mu(K,v)=0,
\]
then 
\[
\mathrm{rot}(\Gamma_v^\varepsilon)=0
\quad\text{and}\quad
tb(\Gamma_v^\varepsilon)=-1.
\]
In particular, $\Gamma_v^\varepsilon$ is Legendrian isotopic to the standard Legendrian unknot.

\smallskip

\noindent
\textup{(ii)} If the polyhedral Maslov index satisfies 
\[
\mu(K,v)\not=0,
\]
then 
\[
\mathrm{rot}(\Gamma_v^\varepsilon)\neq 0,
\]
and therefore $\Gamma_v^\varepsilon$ cannot bound a Lagrangian disk inside $B_\varepsilon(v)$. In particular, the vertex is not locally Lagrangian smoothable.
plain_text: Let $_ v, (S^3,_ std )$ be the Legendrian link obtained by intersecting a locally smoothed valence--$4$ vertex with a sufficiently small sphere. Then the following hold: (i) If the polyhedral Maslov index satisfies \[ (K,v)=0, \] then \[ rot(_v^)=0 tb(_v^)=-1. \] In particular, $_v^$ is Legendrian isotopic to the standard Legendrian unknot. (ii) If the polyhedral Maslov index satisfies \[ (K,v)=0, \] then \[ rot(_v^) 0, \] and therefore $_v^$ cannot bound a Lagrangian disk inside $B_(v)$. In particular, the vertex is not locally Lagrangian smoothable.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Connects polyhedral Maslov index to Legendrian rotation/Thurston–Bennequin invariants and local Lagrangian smoothability; central to symplectic/contact topology with concrete structural consequences for smoothing problems.
viability_rationale: Conjecture stems from LLM heuristics and authors list several delicate technical gaps (Maslov identification, tb computation); resolving requires careful analytic/topological work, making short-term success uncertain.
rationale: The statement is presented as a conjecture (attributed to ChatGPT) and the authors state they could not verify the calculation, listing gaps to be filled for a proof.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture appears as "Conj [ChatGPT 5.2]" with the authors noting "Due to our time budget we could not work to verify this calculation." (lines 548–578, 580–586).

title: New irreducible components of $\mathcal{B}(0,c_2)$ and Computation of the Dimension of its tangent space

For each integer \(b\ 0,1,,k-1\ \) with \(a>b\), there exists a stable rank-2 vector bundle \(E\) arising as the cohomology of a minimal monad of the form equation (a, b,k) : 0 O_ P^ 3 (-a) ^ k matrix O_ P^ 3 (a-1) ^ k O_ P^ 3 (b) \\ \\ O_ P^ 3 (1-a) ^ k _ P^ 3 (-b) matrix O_ P^ 3 (a) ^ k 0. equation Moreover, for all sufficiently large integers \(a 0\), the corresponding family of such bundles belongs to an irreducible component of \(B(0,k(2a-1)-b^2)\), whose dimension exceeds the expected dimension.

title: Nonlinear methods for tensors: determinantal equations for secant varieties beyond cactus

    \[
        \rank\tang^C(T) - n(n-1)(n-2) = dn - \dim\T_{[R]}\Hilb_n\CC^d.
    \]

title: Graph bootstrap percolation -- a discovery of slowness

 [Bollob\'as-Przykucki-Riordan-Sahasrabudhe~\cite{bollobas2017maximum}] The maximum running time for infection rule $K_5$ is $M_{K_5}(n)=o(n^2)$.

title: Ekedahl-Oort Types and Newton Polygons of Abelian Covers of $\mathbf{P}^1$ Branched at Three Points
body_tex:
\label{unlikely-density}
    Let $g \geq 18$, and let $m$ be the least common multiple of the exponents of all of the Galois groups of abelian covers of $\mathbf{P}^1$ branched at three points of genus $g$. If $p$ is a prime such that $p \not \equiv 1$ modulo $m$, then there exist abelian covers $X_1$ and $X_2$ of $\mathbf{P}^1$ branched at three points defined over $\overline{\mathbf{F}_p}$ such that $X_1$ has an unlikely Newton polygon and $X_2$ has an unlikely Ekedahl-Oort type.

evidence_snippet: Conjecture \ref{fanoconjecture}: Let X be a smooth complete intersection of two quadrics... Then there is a semiorthogonal decomposition Db(F_1(X)) = <Db(Sym^2 C), Db(C)^{n-3}, Db(C)^{...}>.

title: Flips for spaces of quadrics on del Pezzo varieties
plain_text: Let $ Q_1, Q_2 $ be a pencil of quadrics in $^ n+2 $ whose base locus is smooth in an odd-dimensional projective space. Then there is a semiorthogonal decomposition equation ((2,)) = (C), , (C)_ n+1 times , (), , ()_ (n-1)(n+1) times . equation
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A relative/pencil version predicting an SOD for orthogonal Grassmannian fibrations; interesting within categorical geometry but a technical, geometry-specific decomposition conjecture.
viability_rationale: Active research area with Kuznetsov/Clifford tools and promised partial progress, but a full relativized semiorthogonal decomposition for all odd dimensions remains nontrivial; moderate short-term solvability likelihood.
rationale: Presented as Conjecture \ref{pencilconjecture} and not proved here; the paper notes it as a natural conjecture and relates it to other open conjectures.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture \ref{pencilconjecture}: For a pencil of quadrics with smooth base locus, Db(OGr(2,\fQ)) = <Db(C)^{n+1}, Db(C)^{(n-1)(n+1)}>. (Stated as a conjecture.)

title: Flips for spaces of quadrics on del Pezzo varieties
plain_text: Let $p : S$ be any quadric fibration of relative dimension $n + 1$ over a smooth base such that the fibers $_s$ are quadrics of rank at least 4, where $n$ is odd. Then there exists a semiorthogonal decomposition equation ((2,)) = (S,_0), , (S,_0)_ n+1 times , (S), , (S)_ (n-1)(n+1) 2 times , equation where $(S,_0)$ is the derived category of the sheaf of even Clifford algebras over the base $S$ associated to the quadric fibration $ S$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Proposes a general SOD for OGr(2, fQ) in terms of even Clifford algebra categories over the base—conceptually broad, linking noncommutative sheaves and geometry with wide applicability to quadric fibrations.
viability_rationale: Reduces to known Kuznetsov cases (point, P^1) and authors report partial progress; approachable by existing derived-category/Clifford-algebra methods but still nontrivial for arbitrary bases—moderate short-term solvability.
rationale: Conjecture \ref{cliffordconjecture} is stated here as an open conjecture (a generalization to quadric fibrations) and is not proved in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture \ref{cliffordconjecture}: For a quadric fibration p: \fQ->S with rank≥4 fibers (n odd), Db(OGr(2,\fQ)) = <Db(S,cl_0)^{n+1}, Db(S)^{(n-1)(n+1)/2}>.

title: On the symplectic capacity and mean width of convex bodies
body_tex:
\label{conj}
 A convex body $K \subset \RR^{2n}$ satisfies 
 $\nlmw(K) =\mw (K)$
if and only if $QK$ is toric for some unitary matrix $Q \in \mathrm{U}(n)$.
plain_text: A convex body $K ^ 2n $ satisfies $(K) = (K)$ if and only if $QK$ is toric for some unitary matrix $Q U(n)$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A structural classification tying symplectic invariance of mean width to toric symmetry; resolving it would clarify deep geometry-symmetry links within symplectic convexity (significant within the subfield).
viability_rationale: Specialized symplectic/convex conjecture with meaningful local progress and stability results; full iff characterization remains nontrivial but seems approachable with further targeted advances.
rationale: The paper states this exact conjecture (Conjecture 3.1) and provides supporting examples and partial/local results but does not prove or refute the if-and-only-if statement.
evidence_snippet: Lines 317-321: "A convex body K ⊂ R^{2n} satisfies nlmw(K)=mw(K) if and only if QK is toric for some unitary matrix Q ∈ U(n)."

title: Almost all graphs are vertex-minor universal
body_tex: $R_{\rm{piv}}(k) \leq \mathsf{poly}(k)$.
plain_text: $R_ piv (k) poly(k)$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A polynomial upper bound for pivot-minor Ramsey numbers is a meaningful combinatorial conjecture with consequences for vertex-/pivot-minor theory, but it is focused to graph minor/Ramsey specialists.
viability_rationale: Authors' random-graph universality and Erdős–Hajnal results give supporting evidence, but best known upper bounds are exponential; proving a polynomial bound likely needs new structural breakthroughs, so plausible but uncertain in five years.
rationale: The authors explicitly conjecture R_{piv}(k) ≤ poly(k) (lines 1160–1162). The paper gives related bounds but does not prove this polynomial upper bound.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1160-1162: "\begin{conjecture} R_{\rm{piv}}(k) \leq \mathsf{poly}(k). \end{conjecture}"

title: Almost all graphs are vertex-minor universal
body_tex: There exists $C\in [\sqrt{2\log_2(4/3)},\sqrt{2\log_2(3)}]$ such that for any $\eps > 0$, with high probability $G\sim \bG(n,1/2)$ is $(C-\eps)\sqrt n$-vertex-minor universal and not $(C+\eps)\sqrt n$-vertex-minor universal.
plain_text: There exists $C [2_2(4/3),2_2(3)]$ such that for any $ > 0$, with high probability $G (n,1/2)$ is $(C-) n$-vertex-minor universal and not $(C+) n$-vertex-minor universal.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Predicts a sharp universality threshold (explicit constant) in G(n,1/2); proving it would be an important probabilistic-combinatorics result about vertex-minor universality and threshold behavior.
viability_rationale: Authors prove matching Θ(√n) bounds (constants ~0.911 and ~1.78). Closing this gap to a single sharp constant likely needs new techniques; moderate chance of resolution within five years given partial results.
rationale: The paper proposes a sharp-threshold constant C for vertex-minor universality in G(n,1/2) (lines 1175–1177) and does not resolve its existence.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1175-1177: "There exists C ∈ [√{2 log_2(4/3)}, √{2 log_2(3)}] such that ... G∼G(n,1/2) is (C−ε)√n-vertex-minor universal and not (C+ε)√n-vertex-minor universal."

title: Almost all graphs are vertex-minor universal
body_tex:
\label{conj:p neq 1/2}
    If $p = \omega(1/\sqrt{n})$ is at most $1/2$ and $G \sim \bG(n,p)$ or $G \sim \bG(n,1-p)$, then $G$ is $k$-vertex-minor universal with high probability for some $k = \Omega(p\sqrt n)$.
plain_text: If $p = (1/n)$ is at most $1/2$ and $G (n,p)$ or $G (n,1-p)$, then $G$ is $k$-vertex-minor universal with high probability for some $k = (p n)$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Natural extension of the main G(n,1/2) result to general p; technically useful and broadens applicability, but mainly a quantitative generalization rather than a conceptual leap.
viability_rationale: p=1/2 case is proved and authors give a concrete spectral/random-walk roadmap; the main obstacle (correlated steps) appears technical and likely surmountable with existing probabilistic tools within a few years.
rationale: The authors conjecture an extension to p≠1/2 (Conjecture 4.3, lines 1181–1183): for p=ω(1/√n) the random graph is k-vertex-minor universal with k=Ω(p√n). It is stated without proof.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1181-1183: "If p = ω(1/√n) ≤ 1/2 and G ∼ G(n,p) or G ∼ G(n,1-p), then G is k-vertex-minor universal w.h.p. for some k = Ω(p√n)."

title: Quantitative partitioned index theorem and noncompact band-width
body_tex:
\label{con band width cpt}
Let $N$ be a compact manifold without boundary, of dimension $n-1 \geq 5$. Suppose that $N$ does not admit Riemannian metrics of positive scalar curvature. Consider a Riemannian metric $g$ on $N \times [-1,1]$. Let $\ell$ be the distance between $N \times \{-1\}$ and $N \times \{1\}$ with respect to this metric. Then
\[
	\inf_{x\in N \times [-1,1]}\Sc_g(x)\le\frac{4\pi^2(n-1)}{n\ell^2}.
\]
plain_text: Let $N$ be a compact manifold without boundary, of dimension $n-1 5$. Suppose that $N$ does not admit Riemannian metrics of positive scalar curvature. Consider a Riemannian metric $g$ on $N [-1,1]$. Let $$ be the distance between $N \ -1\ $ and $N \ 1\ $ with respect to this metric. Then \[ _ x N [-1,1] _g(x)4^2(n-1) n^2 . \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A central conjecture in scalar-curvature geometry (Gromov's band-width). Proving it in full beyond Rosenberg-index cases would have significant structural and rigidity consequences for positive scalar curvature metrics.
viability_rationale: Active partial results (Cecchini, Zeidler) cover Rosenberg-index cases; authors extend techniques to noncompact settings, but full Gromov band-width conjecture remains a broad geometric-analytic challenge, unlikely fully settled in 5 years.
rationale: Gromov's band-width conjecture is stated and remains open in full generality; only partial results (e.g. under nonzero Rosenberg index) have been proved by Cecchini and Zeidler.
evidence_snippet: Lines 229–236 and 239–252: "Gromov made the following conjecture... Versions of Gromov's conjecture were proved by Zeidler and Cecchini (under Rosenberg index nonzero)."

title: On a conjecture by Michael Wemyss regarding the calculation of GV invariants
plain_text: Let $A$ be a contraction algebra and $d = _ A$. Then enumerate a point $[A]$ corresponding to $A$ in the moduli space $_d$ of associative unital algebras (see~) is smooth, in other words, $A$ is unobstructed, the dimension of the centre is constant in an open neighbourhood of $[A]$ and the associated map $A Z(A)$ is smooth. enumerate
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Unobstructedness of contraction algebras would structurally connect noncommutative deformations to commutative theory and enable intrinsic GV invariant calculations, with substantial impact in 3-fold flop and deformation theory.
viability_rationale: Authors proved types A and D; extending to all contraction algebras requires deeper deformation-theoretic control and new tools, plausible but nontrivial.
rationale: The conjecture about unobstructedness and constancy/smoothness of the centre is stated as a new open conjecture; the paper proves cases (types A and D) and small d checks but not the general statement.
evidence_snippet: Lines 149-158, 175-179: 'We formulate this precisely in the following conjecture... Let A be a contraction algebra... [A] is smooth... the dimension of the centre is constant... We verified ... for small values of d ... the conjecture does not follow from vanishing of HH^3(A,A).'

title: The Ising magnetisation field and the Gaussian free field
body_tex:
\label{conj:AT-mag-field}
	 The statement of Theorem~\ref{thm:main-decomp} under the almost verbatim analogue of the iterations \hyperlink{pos}{$(+)$} and \hyperlink{free}{$(\text{free})$} holds for the Ashkin--Teller magnetisation field defined by~\eqref{eq:fielddef} at each point of the critical line~\eqref{eq:critical}, the only difference being that every explicit occurrence of $\sqrt 2$ has to be again replaced with $\sqrt g$ defined in~\eqref{eq:defg}.
plain_text: The statement of Theorem~ under the almost verbatim analogue of the iterations pos $(+)$ and free $(free)$ holds for the Ashkin--Teller magnetisation field defined by~ at each point of the critical line~, the only difference being that every explicit occurrence of $ 2$ has to be again replaced with $ g$ defined in~.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Extends the continuum geometric decomposition of magnetisation fields to the Ashkin–Teller line; important for continuum AT field theory but contingent on other convergence conjectures.
viability_rationale: Follows from Conj. 631 and height-function convergence; authors and others (Aru–Lupu) have partial results, making this moderately likely if prior conjectures are resolved.
rationale: Conjecture~\ref{conj:AT-mag-field} is explicitly posed and the paper only proves partial analytic facts (Proposition~\ref{prop:AT-mag-field}), not the full conjectured decomposition.

title: The Ising magnetisation field and the Gaussian free field
body_tex:
\label{conj:AT-inter}
	Consider the clusters of the decomposition described in Conjecture \ref{conj:AT-IGMC} under $+/+$ boundary conditions. Toss a fair coin for each cluster to assign a value $+1$ or $-1$, except for the boundary cluster which is assigned $+1$. If two clusters with the value $+1$ (resp. $-1$) touch each other, we say that they belong to the same $\oplus$-cluster (resp. $\ominus$-cluster). Then, the law of the outermost interfaces of the Ashkin-Teller magnetisation field is given by the outermost outer boundaries of the $\ominus$-clusters. The full collection of interfaces is defined iteratively.
plain_text: Consider the clusters of the decomposition described in Conjecture under $+/+$ boundary conditions. Toss a fair coin for each cluster to assign a value $+1$ or $-1$, except for the boundary cluster which is assigned $+1$. If two clusters with the value $+1$ (resp. $-1$) touch each other, we say that they belong to the same $$-cluster (resp. $$-cluster). Then, the law of the outermost interfaces of the Ashkin-Teller magnetisation field is given by the outermost outer boundaries of the $$-clusters. The full collection of interfaces is defined iteratively.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Describes a natural continuum percolation construction of AT interfaces via coin-flipped cluster signs; interesting structural picture but narrower and conditional on earlier conjectures.
viability_rationale: More geometric and iterative, relying on earlier conjectures; plausible conditional on those but more speculative and technically delicate, lowering near-term solvability.
rationale: The continuum percolation description of AT interfaces is stated as Conjecture~\ref{conj:AT-inter} and is not proved in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 670–672: 'Then, the law of the outermost interfaces of the Ashkin-Teller magnetisation field is given by the outermost outer boundaries of the \ominus-clusters.' (Conjecture~\ref{conj:AT-inter}.)

title: Tilted Richardson Varieties
body_tex: $\leq_\ba$ is a ranked poset.

interestingness_rationale: Whether the global tilted Bruhat order is ranked is a natural structural question for the new poset family; useful for theory development but largely a local/technical property within the paper's framework.
viability_rationale: Every interval is already shown ranked; promoting this to a global rank function is a concrete, likely technical combinatorial task plausibly solvable in a few years.
rationale: The authors conjecture that the tilted Bruhat order ≤_a is a ranked poset; they note related interval-ranked results but do not prove the global ranked-poset statement.

title: Generalized nearby cycles via relative and logarithmic $\mathscr{D}$-modules
body_tex:
With notation as above, let $\cM$ be a regular holonomic $\shD_X$-module. For $K\subseteq \N^r$ and $\bm\in\N^r$, 
    $$Z(B^{K_\bm}(\cN_0))\subseteq \C^r$$
    is a finite union of translated linear subvarieties. Moreover, if $\cM$ underlies a $\Q$-mixed Hodge module, then   $Z(B^{K_\bm}(\cN_0))\subseteq \C^r$ is a finite union of $\Q$-translated linear subvarieties.

Moreover, if $$ underlies a $$-mixed Hodge module, then $Z(B^ K_ (_0)) ^r$ is a finite union of $$-translated linear subvarieties.

interestingness_rationale: A geometric Budur-type statement linking zero loci of Bernstein–Sato ideals to translated linear subvarieties bridges D-modules, Hodge theory, and singularity theory; structurally meaningful across these areas.
viability_rationale: This is a strong geometric generalization of Budur's conjecture in D-module/Hodge theory; deep and longstanding, with current results giving evidence but not an obvious short-term route to full resolution.

title: Transverse expansion of the metric at null infinity
body_tex:
\label{conjecture}
Let $\{\scri,\bg,\bm\ell,\elltwo,\sigma,\mf q\}$ be $\scri$-structure data of odd dimension $\mf n\ge 7$ admitting a cross-section $\iota:\Sigma \hookrightarrow\scri$ with induced metric $h\d\iota^{\star}\bg$ and let $\mc{O}_{ab}^{\scri}$ be the radiative obstruction tensor. Denote by $\mc{O}^{FG}$ the Fefferman-Graham obstruction tensor of $[h]$. Then, $$\iota^{\star}\big(\text{\pounds}_n^{(\frac{\mf n-3}{2})} \mc{O}^{\scri} \big) = c_{\mf n} \, \mc{O}^{FG},$$ where $c_{\mf n}$ is a constant depending only on $\mf n$.
plain_text: Let $\ ,,,,, q\ $ be $$-structure data of odd dimension $ n 7$ admitting a cross-section $: $ with induced metric $h^ $ and let $O_ ab ^ $ be the radiative obstruction tensor. Denote by $O^ FG $ the Fefferman-Graham obstruction tensor of $[h]$. Then, $$^ (_n^ ( n-3 2 ) O^ ) = c_ n \, O^ FG ,$$ where $c_ n $ is a constant depending only on $ n$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Connects the radiative obstruction at null infinity to the Fefferman–Graham obstruction tensor in higher odd dimensions; supported by low-d evidence and would clarify asymptotic geometric structure in relativity and conformal geometry.
viability_rationale: Strong evidence from lower dimensions and ambient-metric work; conjecture reduces to detailed control of lower-order terms — technical but plausibly resolvable by specialists within a few years.
rationale: The authors pose this relation between the radiative obstruction and Fefferman–Graham tensor as a conjecture and state they intend future work; it is not proved in the paper.
